text: Image quality assessment (IQA) is a long standing topic in computer vision. While many metrics are designed to measure image quality mathematically based on characteristics such as noise and blur, they are all shallow and do not tie the measurements to meaningful words and language. Metrics designed to measure the aesthetic feelings of an image behind its appearance need large amounts of manually annotated training data, which is very time consuming. In this work, we take a different approach based on the fact that there are many visual-language priors learned from massive datasets through pre-training, particularly Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP) models. No task specific fine-tuning is required. With a little bit of engineering including optimal prompt design and their pairwisness, we demonstrate the potential of using pre-trained CLIP for assessing both image quality and also feelings behind an image appearances. Experiments are performed on both designed controlled datasets and widely used benchmarking IQA databases, indicating the power of pre-trained CLIP models that capture a large number of more abstract and human preferences featured visual properties that can well cover a large number of different subjective aesthetic, visual comfort and quality metrics in a universal way.
